variants.hexa_light: In the light world the 16h arm burns as emission - forge-glow rather than painted heat. Freemasonry reads naturally in that register: the blazing eye at the medallion's heart is precisely light as symbol, illumination sought rather than applied. The arm becomes a lamp kept trimmed by discipline.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa star's denser pigment wheel the Mars body keeps its post and temper among eight arms. More points mean finer angles, and angles are the mason's own trade - the square on the medallion exists to test them. Zeal sits comfortably in a geometry that demands more precision, not less.
variants.octa_light: The octa light star is a figure the medallion's compasses could have drawn: an eight-pointed construction of circle and line. Set among its beams, the Mars body's zeal reads as the lodge's ideal - heat converted into illumination, the working eye of the craft opened wider.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mars body stands with the autumn arm, the year's late afternoon - harvest, when work is judged by what it built. For a craft whose emblem is the finished ashlar, autumn is the honest season: the temple is measured and the labor of the bright months weighed.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Mars body sits on the red arm of Love, guarding the night third and sharing it with Venus and Islam. The geometry is on doctrine's side: Brotherly Love is the first of Masonry's three grand principles, and love that stands guard through the night is exactly the fraternal kind.

mercury.name: Taoism
mercury.base:
Taoism follows the Way that cannot be named: water's way, yielding yet unbeatable, the low place all streams obey. The sage acts by not forcing - wu wei - and wins by yielding.

The owner's medallion cuts a taijitu of gold and dark metal into near-black marble: the bright half engraved with scrolling clouds, the dark half with mountains and waves - heaven turning inside earth. Around the rim eight bagua trigrams alternate with small yin-yangs.

It keeps Wednesday because in the East Asian week Wednesday IS the water-star day, and water is the Tao's central metaphor. Its arm hangs at 24h, the purple midnight bottom - and the Tao has always preferred the dark, the valley, the root below.

The arm pairs Wisdom with Greed under the Merchant - a joke the Tao Te Ching enjoys: 'he who knows he has enough is rich.' Across the axis its wisdom curbs Jupiter's excess; at home it simply wants less.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Mercury body wears purple at the midnight bottom - the deepest mix on the painter's wheel, red and blue folded together until they nearly vanish. The Tao would approve of a color made by descent: the dial's lowest arm carrying its most inward pigment.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the midnight arm glows out of the dark, and that is a thoroughly Taoist image: the valley spirit that never dies, light held low. Where pigment absorbs, the luminous arm gives back - darkness used not as absence but as the fertile ground of shining.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa star's eight pigment arms the Mercury body keeps the low seat, and eight is its native count - the medallion's rim already carries the eight bagua trigrams around the turning taijitu. The denser wheel simply completes the circle the trigrams were drawing all along.
variants.octa_light: The octa light star surrounds the hub with eight beams the way the bagua surrounds the taijitu, and that correspondence is the whole point. The Mercury body at the bottom glows quietly among them: the Way does not compete for brightness, and is never outshone.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mercury body holds the winter arm, the year's midnight - and winter is the Tao's home season: sap withdrawn to the root, water resting as ice, strength stored in stillness. What other faiths endure as bleakness, Taoism reads as the return that makes spring possible.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Mercury body sits on the blue arm of Hope, guarding the morning third beside the Moon and Buddhism. Taoist hope is trust without grasping: the empty vessel confident of filling, the winter root sure of spring - hope as patience with the Way's own timing.

jupiter.name: Hinduism
jupiter.base:
Hinduism is the oldest living river of faith: one Brahman behind countless faces, dharma as the order that holds the world, and moksha - release - as the far shore.

The owner's medallion raises a golden Om on amber stone, its curves filled with filigree, the crescent and bindu floating above like the last dot of the sound; the silver rim strings flower rosettes with small Om glyphs set at the quarters.

It keeps Thursday because Thursday is Guruvar - the day of Brihaspati, guru of the gods, who IS Jupiter in Vedic astrology; no mapping on this dial is more literal. Its arm stands at the yellow 12h top, and yellow is what Thursday's devotees actually wear.

The arm pairs Generosity with Excess under the Priest-Teacher, the guru's own calling. Dana, giving, is a pillar of dharma; excess is abundance unmeasured, and the Gita's cure is measure itself - in food, in sleep, in joy.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Jupiter body wears yellow at the noon top - a primary pigment, needing no mixture, like the syllable Om needing no translation. Turmeric and marigold yellow is what Thursday's devotees wear in living practice; the painter's wheel simply agrees with the temple.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the noon arm shines at the dial's crown, one step from the white source. Hindu thought has a name for that adjacency: the medallion's Om is pranava, the sound-form of Brahman - first vibration out from the absolute, as this arm is the first station under the light.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa star's eight pigment arms the Jupiter body keeps the zenith, and eight is auspicious in its world - eight Lakshmis, eight guardians of the directions. The joyful noon body presiding over a fuller wheel is Brihaspati at court: more petals arranged around the same sun.
variants.octa_light: The octa light star spreads eight beams, and Hinduism's noon body crowns them, glowing nearest the source. The diagram is almost Upanishadic: one light, many rays, each ray a valid way back - the faith that says truth is one and the wise call it by many names recognizes itself here.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Jupiter body takes the summer arm, the year's noon - fullness matching its Joy. Festival processions, open temple courts, the feeding of pilgrims by the thousand: abundance is the season's gift, and abundance is exactly what this body's Generosity exists to spend.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Jupiter body sits on the yellow arm of Faith, guarding midday into evening beside Saturn and Judaism. Faith here is shraddha - the trust a student places in a guru - so the teacher's planet carrying the Faith arm is Guruvar stated twice over.

venus.name: Islam
venus.base:
Islam is surrender to the one God - tawhid, the radical oneness of Allah - lived in five daily prayers and in mercy invoked before every act.

The owner's medallion sets a golden crescent on deep lapis blue, its body filled with arabesque vinework - no image of the divine, only pattern, as the tradition insists - and a five-pointed star engraved like a rosette in the crescent's embrace.

It keeps Friday for Jumu'ah, the congregational prayer where the community stands shoulder to shoulder. Its arm is the red 20h evening arm, and in Islam the day begins at sunset - so Venus' hour is no ending but the threshold where each new day and month is born.

The arm pairs Love with Jealousy under the Artist. 'God is beautiful and loves beauty,' the tradition says - the filigree crescent is that saying made metal. Tawhid answers jealousy at the root: all belongs to One, nothing is left to covet.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Venus body wears red at 20h - the primary pigment of passion and warmth. Islamic art knows pigment as devotion: lapis ground with gold for Quran illumination, exactly the palette of the owner's medallion. Red here is love with its sleeves rolled up for the evening prayer.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the evening arm glows the way the maghrib sky does - the hour of the sunset prayer, when the fast breaks and the new day begins. A faith that times itself by actual light, sighting a real crescent to open each month, is at home on a dial where color is luminous.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa star the Venus body joins an eight-pointed figure Islamic art claims as its own: the khatam of two interlocked squares that tiles mosques from Cordoba to Samarkand. For this faith the pigment wheel gaining eight points is not novelty - it is geometry coming home.
variants.octa_light: The octa light star traces eight beams from the hub, and Islamic ornament has drawn that exact figure for a millennium - the eightfold rosette radiating through tile and stone. The Venus body's warmth rendered as pure light suits a faith whose God is named An-Nur, the Light.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Venus body stands with the autumn arm, the year's evening - and Islam has never feared evenings: its day begins at sunset and its months at a crescent barely visible in dusk. Autumn as threshold rather than decline is precisely how this faith counts time.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Venus body sits on the red arm of Love, guarding the night third beside Mars and Freemasonry. Love in Islam is mercy first - Rahman and Rahim, the two names opening every chapter but one - and love keeping watch over the night is the Ramadan vigil in miniature.

saturn.name: Judaism
saturn.base:
Judaism is the covenant faith: one God, a people bound to Him by law and promise, and time itself made holy - its first sanctuary was not a building but a day.

The owner's medallion weaves a golden Star of David from two interlocked triangles on dark slate, the bands inscribed edge to edge with minute Hebrew script - a symbol literally built of text, for a people of the book; silver leaves and small stars circle the rim.

It keeps Saturday because Saturday IS Shabbat: in Hebrew, Saturn is Shabtai, named for the Sabbath itself. Its arm is the green 8h arm of Renewal, and Shabbat is exactly that - the renewal the work-week exists to reach.

The arm pairs Patience with Envy under the Farmer - and Torah farms in sabbaths too: every shmita year the land itself rests as the people do. Patience is covenant kept through long exile; envy is what the tenth commandment names, and forbids.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa the Saturn body wears green at 8h - a mixed pigment, yellow worked into blue, joy tempered with depth. The mixing suits a tradition of argument and interpretation: nothing left simple, everything blended through generations of reading, and still unmistakably itself.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the 8h morning arm glows growing-season green. Judaism keeps its lights literal - Shabbat candles at dusk, the ner tamid never allowed to go out, Hanukkah's eight flames - so a dial where color is kindled rather than painted speaks its native language.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa star's eight pigment arms the Saturn body keeps its patient post, and eight carries weight in its world: covenant on the eighth day, Hanukkah's eight nights - the number just past the week, where holiness exceeds creation. A denser wheel only deepens the fit.
variants.octa_light: The octa light star burns eight beams around the hub, and a Jewish eye cannot see eight lights without seeing Hanukkah - the festival whose flames last exactly that count. The Saturn body among them glows steady and unhurried: patience rendered as the flame that outlasts its oil.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Saturn body stands with the spring arm, and spring is where the Jewish year takes its bearings: Nisan, the month of Passover, is counted first, because liberation is the founding memory. Green Renewal on the spring arm is Exodus retold as a season.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Saturn body sits on the yellow arm of Faith, guarding midday into evening beside Jupiter and Hinduism. Faith here is emunah - steadfastness more than belief - and an arm that holds from noon until candle-lighting at dusk is emunah drawn as geometry.

sun.base:
The Ruler sits enthroned in bronze: a crowned, bearded king with a tall scepter upright in his right hand and a small cross-topped orb resting open in his left palm, an eagle-blazoned shield leaning at the throne's side and imperial eagles engraved into the stone behind him. He is the Sun's child and Sunday's figure — the royal planet's calling, the profession the old woodcuts always drew closest to the light.

On the hexa dial he holds no arm at all: his home is the center, colored white-gold, the sum of every hue on the wheel. That is the star's whole political philosophy in one placement — a ruler is not one calling among six but the point the six radiate from, just as white light is not one color but all of them undivided. The crown works only if every arm can find itself in it.

His virtue is Humility and his vice is Pride, and the image argues the axis by itself: the world he rules is that little orb, small enough to sit in one open hand — held, not gripped. Pride is the center mistaking itself for the source of the light it merely gathers; and since nothing stands opposite the center, the cure cannot come from across the dial. Humility cures Pride — the center heals itself, the only body that must.

That is also why he has no hour. Every mood of the day — noon's Joy, midnight's Sorrow, the Calm before dawn — passes through the throne, and the ruler's discipline is to sit under all of them without belonging to any. The shield rests beside the throne, not on his arm: power at rest, light at its source.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's wheel the Ruler keeps the center of the dial, white-gold, belonging to no hour: the six pigment arms are his light decomposed, every mood radiating from the throne. This is his home reading — the source, not a spoke.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel changes every arm around him but cannot change him: the center stays white-gold, because in the light world it is literally true that white is the sum of all colors. Newton's reading only proves what the throne already claimed.
variants.octa_paint: On the octa star the Ruler steps out of the center onto the 12h arm in pure white (#FFFFFF), taking noon's Joy for his hour. For once the king stands in line with the others — at the top of it, in the one color that still contains all of theirs.
variants.octa_light: On the light octa he holds the noon arm at 12h in green (#00DC00) — the hue at the very peak of human vision, the brightest color the eye can register. Noon's Joy stays his mood; the prism simply names the summit differently.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Ruler stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, its center pointed at the summer solstice — the sun's own festival. He shares the arm with Jupiter: throne and altar on one spoke, king and teacher holding the year's longest light together.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Ruler keeps the center, and the court around him narrows to three arms — Faith yellow, Love red, Hope blue, the painter's primaries. It is the throne's oldest reading made plain: a crown holds only what radiates from it, and all three virtues are his white-gold light taken apart.

moon.base:
The Physician stands mid-diagnosis: a cloaked, bearded man with a lancet raised in one hand and a mortar and pestle cradled in the other, an anatomy book lying open on the lectern beside him, a winged heart and two microscopes engraved into the stone — and the whole scene ringed by a border of serpent and laurel woven into a wreath — the single snake of Asclepius' rod, the healer's oldest animal. He is the Moon's child and Monday's figure: medieval medicine ran on the lunar calendar, timing its bloodletting and its cures by the phases.

His home arm sits at dial 04h in deep blue (#002FFF), the mood called Calm — the hour before dawn. No color fits a healer better: blue is the still, moonlit hour, and the pre-dawn watch is the physician's native shift, the long quiet stretch at the sickbed when the fever decides which way to break.

The Moon's virtue is Serenity and its vice is Fear — the night's two faces, the same darkness lit or unlit — and the image holds both at once: the raised blade heals only if the hand holding it is steady. Straight across the dial at 16h stands the Soldier, and the two professions cure each other: Courage cures Fear, and Serenity quells Wrath. The surgeon must borrow the soldier's nerve; the soldier must borrow the surgeon's calm.

Calm rules the pre-dawn hour because that is when calm is hardest and worth most: the darkest stretch of the night, when the patient is most afraid and the watcher must not be.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 04h arm in deep blue (#002FFF), the Calm of the hour before dawn — the physician's night watch, as the base article tells it.
variants.hexa_light: The prism wheel keeps him at 04h but sharpens the blue to #0040FF — pigment's muted night becomes light's electric one. Same watch, same Calm; the lamp over the sickbed simply burns brighter.
variants.octa_paint: The octa star moves him an hour earlier, to 03h in #005ADC — out of the pre-dawn Calm and into the deep night itself. The watch lengthens: this is the physician at the hardest hour, before any promise of morning.
variants.octa_light: At 03h on the light octa his blue leans violet (#783CF0), borrowing a shade of midnight's Sorrow into the deep night. The healer's color admits what the healer knows: not every night watch ends well.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Physician moves to the 06h arm — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox — and shares it with Saturn, the Farmer. Healer and grower on one spoke, and rightly: both tend living things, and both must wait for them.
variants.trio: The Trinity keeps the Physician at 04h but renames his watch: the blue arm now means Hope, shared with the Merchant, and it bounds the morning from 4h to 12h. No calling earns the word more honestly — hope is the physician's true instrument, the thing he keeps alive at the bedside until the fever breaks.

mars.base:
The Soldier stands square in scale armor, a spear held slantwise in his right hand and a round shield stamped with an eagle on his left arm; crossed spears and axes rise behind his shoulders, a spectacled helm and a battle-axe are engraved in the stone beside him, and the whole ring is wreathed in oak leaves — the tree of endurance, not of triumph. He is Mars' child and Tuesday's figure, the red planet's oldest calling.

His home arm sits at dial 16h in ember orange (#DC9600), the mood called Zeal — the working afternoon. The placement is deliberately unromantic: war in this canon is not the dawn charge but the long grind of the afternoon, fire banked down to a working heat. Orange is Mars' ember — hot enough to forge with, not yet the evening's open flame.

His two hands carry his two faces: the shield is Courage and the spear is Wrath — one fire that defends or destroys, the warrior's virtue and vice being the same heat pointed differently. Across the dial at 04h waits the Physician, and each is the other's medicine: Serenity quells Wrath, Courage cures Fear. The calm hand cools the burning one; the burning heart steadies the frightened one.

Zeal rules the afternoon because that is the soldier's true hour: past noon's glory, before evening's passion — the discipline of continuing after the excitement is gone. Anyone can start a battle at dawn; the soldier is whoever is still holding the line at four.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 16h arm in ember orange (#DC9600), the Zeal of the working afternoon — the banked fire of the base article.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps him at 16h but burns the ember up to pure yellow (#FFFF00) — in light, red and green fused. The working fire glows hotter in Newton's reading, though the hour and its Zeal stand unchanged.
variants.octa_paint: The octa star pulls him an hour earlier, to 15h in #F8E600 — the mid-afternoon, the very middle of the grind. If 16h is the ember, 15h is the anvil hour: no grand mood, just work.
variants.octa_light: At 15h in #DCDC00 the light octa gives him a harder, duller yellow — mid-afternoon light with the shine worked off it. The soldier's least glamorous posting, and therefore his truest.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Soldier stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Venus, the Artist. Mars beside Venus, the oldest pairing in the sky: autumn's red belongs to them both, blood and rose in one color.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Soldier moves to 20h, onto Love's red arm beside the Artist, and the pair bound the night from 20h to 4h. It is the arm that explains the profession: courage is love wearing armor — the soldier stands the night watch not for the fight's sake but for the sake of what sleeps behind him.

mercury.base:
The Merchant sits at his counting table in a fur-trimmed coat and peaked cap, a hand-balance raised in his right fist and a tall staff gripped in his left — Hermes' rod still standing in the trader's hand — with stacks of coins and an open ledger before him and sacks spilling gold at both sides; the border around him is minted from coins, gold and silver, with balance scales set at the quarters. He is Mercury's child and Wednesday's figure: Hermes was the god of merchants and roads.

His home arm sits at dial 0h — midnight, the bottom of the wheel — in deep purple (#542E85), the mood called Sorrow. The color is history's private joke on him: Tyrian purple was the costliest pigment of the ancient world, worth more than the gold in his sacks — a color that was itself a trade empire. And midnight is the accounting hour: the day is over, the market closed, and what remains is to weigh what it was worth.

The scale in his raised hand is the whole ethics of the arm: Wisdom is knowing the true weight of things; Greed is wanting the balance to tip. Straight up the axis at noon stands the Priest, and the two correct each other across the dial: Generosity cures Greed, and Wisdom curbs Excess — the open hand teaching the closed one, the careful scale steadying the overflowing table.

Sorrow rules midnight because every honest ledger closes in it: the day's grave, where profit and loss are finally told apart. The merchant is the one calling that must look at the bottom of the wheel nightly — and wisdom begins exactly there.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the midnight arm at 0h in Tyrian purple (#542E85), the Sorrow of the closed ledger — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his purple to magenta (#BD00BD) — the one hue found in no rainbow, a color the eye invents where the spectrum's two ends meet. Fitting for Mercury, the mind itself: at midnight he wears a color that exists only in thought.
variants.octa_paint: The octa star lifts him off the bottom to 21h in dark red (#DC0000) — the late evening, the market's true closing hour. The reckoning comes earlier here, done by lamplight rather than in the grave of midnight.
variants.octa_light: At 21h in pure red (#FF0000) the light octa gives the late evening its full signal color — the last hot hour before the books close. The scale weighs the same; the lamp is redder.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Merchant keeps the bottom arm at 0h — Winter, blue, pointed at the winter solstice — and he alone shares it with no one. The year's midnight belongs to a single figure: the one calling that can sit at the bottom of the wheel, alone, and still balance the books.
variants.trio: The Trinity lifts the Merchant off midnight to 04h, onto Hope's blue arm beside the Physician, bounding the morning from 4h to 12h. The move names the trade's true engine: every venture is hope with numbers on it — the ledger closed in sorrow at midnight reopens at the morning's gate, credit extended to the day.

jupiter.base:
The Priest — the Teacher — stands robed to the ground, his right hand lifting a staff crowned with a radiant sun above his own head, his left holding an open book turned outward for others to read; a small stone altar stands beside him, a bare tree and whole flocks of birds are cut into the stone behind him, and open books repeat around the border. He is Jupiter's child and Thursday's figure — in the Vedic week Thursday is Guruvar, the day of Brihaspati, who IS Jupiter: the guru of the gods.

His home arm is the top of the dial, 12h, in bright yellow (#F8E600), the mood called Joy — and 'jovial' literally means 'of Jupiter'. The image explains the placement by itself: he raises a sun on a staff because that is what teaching is — lifting the light higher than your own head so that it falls on someone else. Noon is the hour when light is given most freely, and yellow is that gift as a color.

His virtue is Generosity — the book held open and outward, knowledge given away — and his vice is Excess, the royal table overflowing, the blessing that becomes a flood. Straight down the axis at midnight sits the Merchant, and each cures the other: Wisdom curbs Excess, and Generosity cures Greed — the scale below teaching measure to the open hand above.

Joy rules noon because noon is the one hour that needs no lamp: the day's peak, when the sun — like the teacher — asks nothing back for its light.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the top arm at 12h in noon yellow (#F8E600), the Joy of the day's peak — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his noon to green (#00DC00) — the wavelength at the summit of human sight, the brightest color the eye knows. The teacher's hour keeps its Joy; light simply measures the peak instead of painting it.
variants.octa_paint: The octa star moves him to 09h in green (#00DC00) — the mid-morning, which is to say the school hour. The teacher leaves the pulpit's noon for the classroom's morning, and the color of growth fits the work of growing minds.
variants.octa_light: At 09h in cyan (#00DCDC) the light octa gives his mid-morning the color of a clear early sky. The lesson moves nearer to dawn — generosity practiced early, before the day has anything to repay it with.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Priest stands at 12h on the Summer arm, yellow, pointed at the summer solstice — and shares it with the Sun itself. Teacher beside king on the year's highest spoke: the light and the one who lifts it, on the same arm at last.
variants.trio: On the Trinity the Priest keeps the top arm at 12h, yellow — but the yellow now names Faith, his own working material, and he shares the arm with the Farmer as they bound the day from 12h to 20h. The placement reads itself: faith is the sun he lifts on the staff, raised higher than his own head so others can see by it.

venus.base:
The Artist is the only figure on the whole dial who carries actual color: in a world of engraved bronze, the palette in his raised hand bears real dabs of red, blue, yellow and green. A young, long-haired figure in an embroidered doublet and flowing cape, he touches a brush to the palette while a small hammer waits in his lowered hand and a scroll with a quill is tucked at his belt; behind him the comedy and tragedy masks — one laughing, one weeping — hang beside an engraved lyre. He is Venus' child and Friday's figure: in the old woodcuts the planet's children are the musicians and the lovers.

His home arm sits at dial 20h in deep red (#B60000), the mood called Passion — the evening, when Venus herself rises as the evening star. The hour fits the calling exactly: evening is when work releases the heart, when the theatre lights come up, when the day's material world softens into its emotional one. Red is that hour made pigment.

The two masks behind him are his virtue and his vice wearing faces: Love, and Jealousy — the fear of losing what is loved, love's own shadow rather than its opposite. Across the dial at 08h stands the Farmer, and the two heal each other: Patience heals Jealousy, and Love dissolves Envy — the sower teaches the lover to wait, and the lover teaches the sower to cherish his own field rather than covet another's.

Passion rules the evening because passion is what the daylight was for: the day earns, the evening feels. And the palette explains why this figure alone receives color — love, like art, is the moment the world stops being bronze.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 20h arm in deep red (#B60000), the Passion of the evening — the base reading, the evening star's own hour.
variants.hexa_light: The prism keeps him at 20h but purifies the red to its light primary (#FF0000) — the reddest red there is, unmixed. Passion in Newton's world is not a pigment but a wavelength, and the evening burns in it.
variants.octa_paint: The octa star draws the Artist back to 18h in orange (#DC9600) — the dusk arm — and the mood changes with the place: from Passion to Longing, the feeling that looks back. It is the artist's other native hour, since half of art is memory.
variants.octa_light: At 18h in bright orange (#FF8C00) the light octa paints the dusk sky itself — the burning west at sunset. Longing keeps the arm: the day is ending, and the artist is the one who refuses to let it go unrecorded.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Artist stands at 18h — Autumn, red, pointed at the autumnal equinox — sharing the spoke with Mars, the Soldier. Venus beside Mars, as in every myth: autumn's red is harvest and blood and rose at once, and it takes both figures to say so.
variants.trio: The Artist does not move: the Trinity's Love arm is his own 20h in his own red, for love is his virtue in both sets at once. The Soldier joins him, and together they bound the night from 20h to 4h — guard and poet of the same dark hours, one keeping what is loved safe, the other keeping it in color.

saturn.base:
The Farmer stands in rough, fringed cloth with a net slung across his shoulders, a sickle and a bundle of wheat ears raised in his right hand and a shepherd's crook in his left; a plough and an axe lie at his feet, a ram and an ox rest behind him, and the whole scene is wreathed in a border braided from wheat. He is Saturn's child and Saturday's figure — and the sickle in his fist is Cronus' own blade, the harvest god's tool handed down to the harvest man.

His home arm sits at dial 08h in field green (#007E00), the mood called Renewal — the morning. The hour is his by right of habit: the farmer is the calling that rises with it, and green is dew and growth, the week renewing itself on the day of rest.

His color holds his virtue and his vice in a single hue: green is growth — Patience, the sower who waits a season for what he planted — and green is also envy, the bitter green of the neighbor's better harvest. One color, two faces, exactly like the arm itself. Across the dial at 20h stands the Artist, and each cures the other: Love dissolves Envy, and Patience heals Jealousy — the lover teaches the farmer to love his own field, the farmer teaches the lover that what is worth having is worth waiting for.

Renewal rules the morning because the farmer's whole faith lives there: every morning the field is slightly different, and every seed is a wager that waiting works.

variants.hexa_paint: Home position: the 08h arm in field green (#007E00), the Renewal of the morning — the base reading.
variants.hexa_light: The prism turns his morning green to cyan (#00DDDD) — the color of the clear early sky rather than of the field. Renewal keeps the hour; in the light world the farmer looks up before he looks down.
variants.octa_paint: The octa star moves him to the dawn arm itself, 06h in cyan (#00DCDC), and the mood becomes Hope — dawn's own. No calling knows dawn-hope like the sower: every seed is a wager that tomorrow comes, and here he stands on the very arm that faces it.
variants.octa_light: At 06h in sky blue (#0078DC) the light octa deepens the dawn: Hope in a bluer key, the last of night still in the air as the farmer walks out. The wager is the same; the light is younger.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Farmer stands at 06h — Spring, green, pointed at the vernal equinox: sowing season, his own festival. He shares the spoke with the Moon, the Physician — grower and healer together, the two patient professions that tend life and wait for it.
variants.trio: The Trinity promotes the Farmer from the morning to the crown: the 12h Faith arm, yellow, shared with the Priest, bounding the day from 12h to 20h. The pairing is the oldest sermon there is — sowing is faith performed with the hands, every seed a creed: buried, waited on, and believed in before it is seen.

moon.name: Druidism
moon.base:
Druidism is the old faith of the Celtic lands and its modern rebirth: a religion of groves, wells and turning seasons, whose scripture is the living land itself. The druid was priest, healer, judge, keeper of memory.

The owner's medallion shows that trust: a golden Tree of Life whose roots braid downward as its branches climb, a green light glowing in the trunk's heart, ringed by silver Celtic knotwork threaded with red-capped fly agaric mushrooms.

It keeps Monday because druids counted time by the moon - nights before days, months before years; Pliny set the mistletoe rite on the sixth day of the moon. It rides the Moon's blue arm at hour 4, the still hour before dawn.

The arm's pair is the grove's own lesson: Fear is the dark wood seen from outside, Serenity the same wood known from within as kin - fitting for the Physician's arm, whose druid emblem was mistletoe, the all-heal.

variants.hexa_paint: On the painter's hexagram the Moon body wears blue, one of the three pigment primaries - unmixed, original, a color no palette can compose. Pigment is druidry's world: woad from leaves, ochre from earth, the sacred ground by hand from the land itself.
variants.hexa_light: In Newton's world the Moon keeps blue, for blue is a primary in both wheels - pigment and beam agree on it. Yet here it is no longer woad crushed from leaves but moonlight itself: cold, direct, the very light by which druids counted their nights, their months and their tides.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-point star the wheel is cut finer, and the Moon body holds its calm station on the dawn side of the dial. In pigment that calm is deep and absorbent, night water in a sacred well - the extra arms only widen the circle the grove has always stood in.
variants.octa_light: The luminous octagram adds arms the way a stone circle adds sightlines - more risings to watch for. Among eight rays the Moon body keeps its cool, serene glow, reading as moonlight on tidewater: reflected, patient, marking time without ever burning it.
variants.cross: The cross pointer marks the year's four hinges, and the Moon body at hour 4 flanks the dawn arm - the spring equinox side, shared with Saturn's body. For druids this is Alban Eilir, the Light of the Earth: day and night in equal measure, the balance the whole grove-faith keeps.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Moon body stands on the Hope arm, deep blue, guarding the morning third from 4h to 12h and sharing the arm with Mercury. Hope is the druid's horizon virtue: the moon always returns and the wheel always turns - night, in this faith, is a promise rather than an end.

mars.name: Zoroastrianism
mars.base:
Zoroastrianism is among the oldest prophetic faiths: Zarathustra taught that the world is a battlefield between asha - truth, order, light - and the lie, with everyone enlisted through good thoughts, good words, good deeds.

The owner's medallion sets the golden Faravahar on night-dark stone: a crowned, bearded figure rising from a winged disc, one hand raised, the other holding the ring of covenant, inside a silver rim engraved with small flames.

It takes Tuesday because fire is its altar: Atar burns on the fiery planet's day, on the orange arm at hour 16 beside Ares, Tyr and the Soldier - a soldierly faith, every believer a volunteer in truth's war.

Its arm's Courage is asha itself, the nerve to tell the truth; its Wrath is fire's own vice, the tended flame turned wildfire. A religion that has kept single temple fires alight for a thousand years knows the difference exactly.

variants.hexa_paint: On the pigment hexagram the Mars body burns orange, the painter's fire: red passion folded into yellow joy to make ember. Paint suits a religion of tended flame - color as something material, kept and fed, like the temple fires priests have nourished with sandalwood for centuries.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world colors are not mixed but emitted, and the Mars body glows rather than smolders - fire as radiance instead of ember. That is the Zoroastrian reading of flame: Atar is not burning matter but the visible presence of truth, the beam by which asha is seen.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-point star the Mars body keeps its zealous post on the afternoon side of the dial, the day's fighting hours. In pigment that zeal stays martial and warm, a banner color, an ember carried on the march - and this faith has always known which way it faces: toward the fire.
variants.octa_light: The luminous octagram spreads eight rays, and the Mars body holds its own among them like a watchfire in a line of beacons. In light its zeal turns clean and directional - the fire temple's flame seen across the dark - courage that shows the road instead of scorching it.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mars body at hour 16 flanks the dusk arm - the autumn equinox side, shared with Venus. That is Mehregan, the feast of Mithra, lord of covenant and judge of oaths: a season of reckoning fit for a faith where every soul must cross the bridge of its own deeds.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Mars body stands on the Love arm, deep red, guarding the night from 20h to 4h beside Venus. In Zoroastrian terms that love is devotion, Spenta Armaiti - the fire fed through the dark watches; choosing the good again each night is how this faith loves.

mercury.name: Shamanism
mercury.base:
Shamanism is humanity's oldest religious craft: by drum, fast or vision the shaman leaves the body, travels the world-tree and bargains with spirits for healing and lost souls. The word is Evenki; the practice, worldwide.

The owner's medallion glows like a fire in a dark tent: a totem of thunderbird, wolf and bear stacked atop a round hide drum laced with crossed cords, ember light rising beneath, ringed in pewter feathers and antlers.

It takes Wednesday, the messenger's day: the shaman is Mercury's kind of traveler. Odin, this arm's god, hung nine nights on the tree to seize the runes - a shamanic initiation. The purple arm at hour 24 marks midnight, when the drum opens the door.

The arm's Wisdom is bought with ordeal and only real when given away; its Greed is the shaman's corruption - hoarding the spirits' gifts, selling the crossing. Fittingly, the Merchant works the same arm.

variants.hexa_paint: On the pigment hexagram the Mercury body wears purple, red and blue married into a between-color - the shaman's exact address, neither this world nor the other. In paint the hue is earthly and deep, pigment smeared on a drumhead or blown against a cave wall ten thousand years ago.
variants.hexa_light: In the world of light the purple reach of the wheel grows stranger: where red and blue beams meet lies a color no rainbow contains, a hue the eye invents at the spectrum's closed seam. A fitting banner for the walker between worlds - the shaman stands where the visible loops back on itself.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-point star the Mercury body keeps midnight, the dial's lowest point, under the silver tip rather than the gold. In pigment its sorrow is dusk-deep, the color of the descent. Eight arms simply add doors, and the shaman is the one figure on the dial who knows how to open them.
variants.octa_light: The luminous octagram turns the star into eight rays, and the Mercury body holds the midnight ray - light at the very bottom of the night. That is the drum journey itself: a glow carried down into darkness and brought back up, sorrow shining like phosphorescence that needs no sun.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Mercury body stands at hour 24, on the winter solstice arm itself - the longest night, silver-tipped at the dial's foot. Winter is the shaman's season: the world sleeps, the tent closes, the drum begins, and the deepest journeys are made while the sun is furthest away.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Mercury body stands on the Hope arm, deep blue, guarding the morning from 4h to 12h beside the Moon. Hope is the shaman's entire errand: to go down after the lost and return with them by dawn. Every journey wagers that something can be brought back.

jupiter.name: Sikhism
jupiter.base:
Sikhism, founded by Guru Nanak in fifteenth-century Punjab, teaches one formless God, equality of all, honest work and sharing - ten Gurus completed by the scripture itself, enthroned as the living Guru.

The owner's medallion raises the golden Khanda against a starry midnight field: the double-edged sword upright in the chakkar's ring, two curved kirpans crossed behind it, in a silver rim of alternating lotus flowers and khanda emblems.

It owns Thursday by name: Guruvar, the Guru's day - and Jupiter is Brihaspati, guru of the gods. It stands at hour 12 in yellow, the dial's noon, on the Priest and Teacher's arm, its Joy the mood of a langar hall at midday.

The arm's Generosity is a pillar of the faith - vand chhako, share what you earn - and its Excess is what the Gurus' discipline trims away. The Khanda cuts both ways: abundance can feed or bloat; the Sikh keeps it a service.

variants.hexa_paint: On the pigment hexagram the Jupiter body wears yellow, a painter's primary - and nearly the color Sikhs already fly: the kesri saffron of the Nishan Sahib above every gurdwara. In paint the hue is ground and given like grain, a harvest color, the shade of food offered rather than kept.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world yellow is not a primary but a sum: red and green beams meeting make it. That suits Sikh teaching - the brightest things are joined things, sangat over solitude, the shared langar over the lone meal. Yellow lives in both wheels, as the faith lives in both work and prayer.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-point star the Jupiter body keeps high noon, the top of the dial nearest its golden tip. In pigment its joy is granary-warm, the mood of a langar hall at midday. However finely the wheel is cut, this body does not move: generosity stands where the sun stands highest.
variants.octa_light: The luminous octagram fans out eight rays, and the Jupiter body crowns the noon ray. In light its yellow mood becomes sheer radiance, the same glow the owner painted around the Khanda - the Name remembered at the brightest hour, a light that exists only by being given off.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Jupiter body stands at hour 12, on the summer solstice arm, beneath the gold of the dial's crown. High summer is the year's own langar: the sun giving its utmost without keeping accounts - and Vaisakhi, the harvest feast, is the day the Khalsa itself was born.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Jupiter body carries the Faith arm itself, bright yellow, guarding the day from 12h to 20h beside Saturn. Faith is this religion's root word: sikh means learner, one who trusts the Guru enough to be taught - belief practiced daily as work, remembrance and sharing.

saturn.name: Voodoo
saturn.base:
Vodou was born in Haiti when West African religion survived the slave ships, wearing Catholic saints as masks: one creator, Bondye, served through the lwa, spirits who ride their people in ceremony, and the ancestors.

The owner's medallion restores dignity to the emblem outsiders know best: a burlap doll with a copper skull face, chest bound in an X of twine, cowries ringing the silver rim, and a copper belly plate engraved with a veve, the sign that calls a spirit down.

It rules Saturday through Baron Samedi - 'Baron Saturday' - keeper of the crossroads and the dead, at home on Saturn's green arm at hour 8, the Farmer's arm: the Ghede govern graves and fertility alike.

The arm's Patience is the ancestor-faith itself, debts honored over generations; its Envy is the sorcery Vodou is slandered with and its ethics condemn. Green Renewal is its truth: nothing sprouts that was not buried.

variants.hexa_paint: On the pigment hexagram the Saturn body wears green, the painter's mix of yellow and blue - growth out of dark ground. Pigment is Vodou's own medium: veve traced in cornmeal and ash on the temple floor, color poured onto earth to open a way. This green is graveyard grass, life fed by the dead.
variants.hexa_light: In the world of light green turns primary, one of the three beams that sum to white. There is a quiet justice in that: a faith of the enslaved, long dismissed as darkness, holds a primary seat in the brighter wheel. Green light is the Ghede's paradox made visible - out of the tomb, the most living color.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-point star the Saturn body keeps its post on the rising side of the dial, its mood of renewal intact. In pigment that renewal is loamy and slow, compost color, the patience of soil. Eight arms cut more crossroads into the dial - and every crossroads in Vodou belongs first to the keeper of the gate.
variants.octa_light: The luminous octagram fans out eight rays, and the Saturn body holds its calm renewal among them like a candle set on a grave at Fet Gede. In light the mood turns from soil to flame-glow: small emitted brightnesses insisting that the dead are not gone, only standing elsewhere on the wheel.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross the Saturn body at hour 8 flanks the dawn arm - the spring equinox side, shared with the Moon. Spring is this faith's deepest teaching made season: the Ghede who rule the grave rule fertility too, and nothing sprouts that was not first buried. Dawn out of the cemetery is Vodou's whole hope.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Saturn body stands on the Faith arm, bright yellow, guarding 12h to 20h beside Jupiter. Vodou's faith is service, sevis lwa - a reciprocal bond: feed the spirits and they hold your world steady. Even the Baron, joking at death's gate, is trusted utterly to open it.

sun.name: Ancient Egyptian religion (Egypt)
sun.base:
The religion of ancient Egypt watched the sky for three thousand years and set the Sun at the heart of everything: Ra sailed his barque across the day, grew old by dusk, died into the underworld and was reborn every dawn - the oldest telling of the cycle this dial turns through.

The owner's medallion gives the god in gold: the falcon-headed Ra-Horakhty in the striped royal headdress, the blazing sun disc above his brow encircled by the cobra Wadjet, hieroglyphs running down his collar; the rim weaves ankhs - the key of life - with the Eye of Horus, gold on silver over lapis blue.

It guards Sunday as the day's eldest owner: no faith wove the Sun so completely into kingship, temple and tomb, and the white-gold CENTER of the dial - the source all six colors decompose from - is precisely where Egypt put Ra, the sum the other gods flowed into and out of.

The center pairs Humility with Pride, and Egypt staged that axis as no one else: pharaohs called themselves sons of Ra, yet every soul - royal or not - ended before the scales, its heart weighed against the feather of Ma'at. Pride built the pyramids; humility was the feather they were answerable to.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa Egypt holds the unmixable white-gold center. Pigment cannot blend its way to white - and Egyptian art knew it: tomb painters kept their gold as leaf, laid on, not mixed. The source is given from above, which is the whole Ra theology in a painter's fact.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world six beams sum to white at the hub, and Egypt's seat becomes literal physics: Ra at dawn pouring out every color the day will use. The medallion's radiant disc over the falcon's head is exactly this - emission, not surface.
variants.octa_paint: On the eight-pointed octa wheel Egypt would smile at the count: eight were the primordial gods of Hermopolis, the Ogdoad, who existed before the first sunrise. Eight pigment arms around the hub, and the Sun still breaks from their middle - creation retold in paint.
variants.octa_light: The octa light star rings the hub with eight beams, and Egypt read sunbeams as hands: Akhenaten's Aten reached down with little palms at the end of every ray, light literally giving life. Eight glowing arms from a white center is an Amarna relief drawn by a clock.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Egypt keeps the crossing where the four arms meet. Its own year knew three seasons - flood, sowing, harvest, all timed by the Nile and the rising of Sirius - but every one of them was counted from the Sun's house: the center, where this faith always stood.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer the Sun keeps the center and the theme of Faith rises vertically from it - and Egypt built that vertical in stone: the obelisk is a petrified sunbeam, a single shaft of Ra planted in the earth. Faith, Hope and Love fan out from the same disc his falcon carries.

venus.name: Babylonian religion (Babylon)
venus.base:
Babylon's faith made the planets gods and the gods mathematics: its priests watched Venus from ziggurat tops, pressed her risings into clay, and named the seven days after the seven wanderers - the very week this clock still keeps was Babylon's invention.

The owner's medallion is Ishtar entire: her eight-pointed star in burning copper, cuneiform running down the rays, the sign of Venus set in its heart; beneath it strides her lion - the same beast that paced the Processional Way in glazed brick - and the silver rim parades lions' heads between palm fronds on a starry lapis night.

It guards Friday by identity, not analogy: Ishtar IS Venus, morning and evening star, and Friday is Venus' day in every tongue that kept the planetary week. The red 20h arm, Passion's hour, is her exact address - the evening star lighting the first hour of the night.

The arm pairs Love with Jealousy, and no goddess carried both more openly: goddess of love AND war in one body, who, refused by Gilgamesh, loosed the Bull of Heaven on his city. The Artist shares her arm - and Babylon's artists answered her with the most beautiful bricks ever fired.

variants.hexa_paint: On the paint hexa Babylon wears the deep red of Venus' 20h arm as pigment - kiln-fired, like the city itself: glazed brick reds and golds burned permanent in the Ishtar Gate. Love as a material you can build walls from, jealousy as the same fire left untended.
variants.hexa_light: In the light world the red arm becomes emission - and Ishtar was always light first: the evening star, the one lamp lit before all others in the darkening sky. Her medallion star glows copper against lapis night exactly as Venus does at dusk.
variants.octa_paint: The octa pointer is Babylon's own emblem drawn large: the star of Ishtar has EIGHT points, and here the whole dial wears her sign. On the pigment wheel her body keeps Venus' seat among the eight arms - the goddess recognizing her own geometry.
variants.octa_light: Eight beams around the hub - the light octa renders Ishtar's eight-pointed star as radiance, which is how Babylon actually met her: not as paint but as the brightest wanderer, rising and setting on schedules her astronomers could finally predict.
variants.cross: On the seasons cross Venus' body shares the autumn arm with Mars - love and war side by side, which for Babylon is no pairing at all but one goddess: Ishtar owned both. Autumn suits her too - the harvest festivals when the evening star stood longest over the city.
variants.trio: On the Trinity pointer her body stands on the red arm of Love, paired with Mars - and Babylon would call that arm simply 'Ishtar', love and war in one body, guarding the night third where the evening star reigns. Love as the theology of the first hour of darkness.

Aries.base:
Aries opens the zodiac at the point astronomers still call the First Point of Aries, the spring equinox. On the year wheel it begins at 270 degrees, the left side of the dial, the year's own six in the morning, and guards 21 March to 19 April, when everything begins again.

The owner paints the ram rearing inside a dark disc, forelegs lifted in mid-leap, one horn wound into a full spiral, while the constellation is only a bent line of a few stars with one bright bend. So much force spent on so modest a piece of sky.

It rides the green arm of Renewal, Saturday's arm borrowed from Saturn, where the virtue is Patience and the shadow is Envy: a hard school for a headlong ram whose ruler Mars burns far away on the orange arm of Tuesday. Its arm-mate Taurus walks the same green path at a slower, steadier pace.

variants.paint: In the paint world Aries wears deep pigment green, the painter's mixture of yellow and blue. It is the green of first grass and wet spring earth, a color made of matter, and it grounds the ram: fire held inside growing things, impatience buried in a seed that must wait for its season.
variants.light: In the light world the spring arm glows cyan, green and blue light added together on the screen. The ram turns electric and cool, dawn air instead of soil, and its charge is no longer buried in earth but broadcast as pure signal, renewal read as energy rather than matter.

Taurus.base:
Taurus is the bull of the oldest sky, remembered as the white shape Zeus wore to carry Europa across the sea. It guards 20 April to 20 May, the arc from 300 to 330 degrees, the heart of spring when the year climbs steadily toward its summit at the top of the dial.

The owner's bull stands inside a dark disc with its head raised and one foreleg lifted, horns curving upward, all chest and haunch. The constellation repeats those horns as a long open fork of stars, two bright tips diverging from the cluster at the bull's eye.

It stands on the green arm of Renewal, Saturday's arm under Saturn, teaching Patience against the vice of Envy, and no sign wears that lesson more naturally than the unhurried bull. Its ruler Venus keeps house on the red arm of Friday, and its arm-mate Aries supplies the spark that Taurus turns into steady ground.

variants.paint: In pigment Taurus wears deep green, yellow and blue ground together into the color of pasture and leaf. The subtractive world of matter is the bull's own element: color you can till, weigh and hold, renewal as something grown slowly out of soil rather than switched on.
variants.light: In the prism world the bull's arm shines cyan, blue and green light summed on the screen. The pasture becomes clear water and cold sky, and Taurus grows lighter than itself: the same patience, but read as a calm transparent frequency instead of heavy fertile earth.

Gemini.base:
Gemini is Castor and Pollux, one twin mortal and one divine, and the myth turns on a gift: Pollux split his immortality so neither brother would be lost alone. The sky keeps them as two parallel chains of stars, two figures standing side by side.

The owner draws them as twin women in long gowns, hands joined between them, each raising a leafy sprig in her free hand, mirrored like a reflection. The glyph is two pillars bridged at top and bottom, the Roman two.

Gemini guards 21 May to 20 June, the arc from 330 degrees to the very top of the dial, spring ripening into summer. It sits on the yellow arm of Joy, Thursday's arm under Jupiter, whose virtue is Generosity and whose vice is Excess, the exact moral of its own myth. Cancer shares that arm just across the solstice, and Gemini's ruler Mercury lives on the purple arm of Wednesday.

variants.paint: In the paint world Gemini wears pure pigment yellow, a painter's primary that no mixing can produce. That suits the twins: something first-hand and unmixed, the brightest matter on the palette, joy laid on thick before any other color has touched it.
variants.light: In the light world the noon arm turns pure green, a primary of the prism as yellow is of the palette. Gemini keeps its rank but changes its voice: primary in both worlds, now glowing at the eye's most sensitive wavelength, quick conversation carried as living signal.

Cancer.base:
Cancer begins exactly at the top of the dial: its first point is the summer solstice, the anchor on which DOMY's whole year wheel is pinned, and the Tropic of Cancer still carries its name. It guards 21 June to 22 July, the first arc of high summer, 0 to 30 degrees.

The myth is small and stubborn: the crab Hera sent to pinch Heracles, crushed underfoot and set among the faintest stars of the zodiac, a dim slender Y in the sky. The owner restores its dignity, drawing the crab from above with both pincers raised open and eight legs fanned out in perfect symmetry.

It sits on the yellow arm of Joy, Thursday's arm under Jupiter, learning Generosity and shadowed by Excess, with Gemini as its neighbor on the same arm across the solstice. Its ruler is the Moon, at home on the blue arm of Monday: a child of night standing guard at the year's brightest gate.

variants.paint: In pigment Cancer wears primary yellow, the brightest matter a brush can carry. There is a tenderness in giving the Moon's shy creature the sunniest paint on the wheel: the crab keeps its shell, but Thursday's yellow warms it into open-handed, generous gold.
variants.light: In the light world Cancer's arm shines pure green light at the top of the star. The moonchild trades gold for the prism's own primary, and its softness turns luminous: not the glare of noon but the green of things that grow through it, joy as emitted light.

Leo.base:
Leo is the Nemean lion, the first labor of Heracles, whose hide no weapon could pierce, set into the sky with Regulus, the little king, burning at its heart. It guards 23 July to 22 August, the arc from 30 to 60 degrees, the furnace weeks just past the solstice.

The owner's lion strides in profile, mane flaring like fire around its lifted head, tail carried in a long S with a tufted tip. The stars draw the same mane as the Sickle, a backwards question mark curling above the body.

It burns on the orange arm of Zeal, Tuesday's arm under Mars, where the virtue is Courage and the vice is Wrath, the lion's two temperatures. Virgo shares that arm as its quieter half. And Leo's ruler is unique: the Sun does not live on any arm but at the very center of the dial, the white light of Sunday from which all six arm colors are decomposed. The lion answers to the source itself.

variants.paint: In pigment Leo wears burnt orange, red and yellow milled together into the color of embers and late July. It is a mixed color for a proud sign, and that is its lesson: even the lion's fire is composed, courage blended from passion and joy, matter warmed rather than burning.
variants.light: In the light world Leo's arm blazes pure yellow, red and green light added until they sum toward the Sun's own brightness. Nothing is milled or muted here; the lion becomes a lamp, zeal as radiation, the closest of the six lights to the white center it answers to.

Virgo.base:
Virgo is the star-maiden, Astraea who left the earth last of the immortals, or Demeter of the grain; either way the sky agrees, hanging Spica, the ear of wheat, as the bright star in her hand. She guards 23 August to 22 September, from 60 degrees down to the autumn equinox at 90, closing the summer at the dial's right side, the year's dusk.

The owner draws her winged, head bowed so her long hair veils her face, a sprig held up in one hand, her gown falling in folds; the constellation sprawls wide around that bright hand-star.

She stands, surprisingly, on the orange arm of Zeal, Tuesday's arm under Mars, beside Leo, her louder arm-mate. The pairing is the lesson: the arm's virtue is Courage and its vice is Wrath, and Virgo shows courage in its harvest form, exact, patient and unarmed. Her ruler Mercury keeps its home on the purple arm of Wednesday.

variants.paint: In pigment Virgo wears harvest orange, red and yellow mixed into terracotta and ripe grain. The subtractive world suits her: a color of clay, bread and September fields, zeal made useful, the soldier's hue mellowed into everything the soldier fights to protect.
variants.light: In the light world her arm turns pure yellow, red and green light summed brighter than either. The gleaner becomes lucid: precision as illumination, the lamp on the desk rather than the fire in the field, her quiet zeal emitted steadily at the screen's warmest primary.

Libra.base:
Libra is the only sign that is an object rather than a creature: a balance. It opens on 23 September at the autumn equinox, when day and night stand exactly equal and the year performs its own act of weighing, and it holds the wheel from 90 to 120 degrees, easing the light down into October.

The owner draws the idea honestly: a man on one knee, one arm raised, holding the two pans level from a single point of the beam. The glyph below is a sun resting on the horizon line, neither risen nor set.

It rides the red Friday arm of Venus, whose mood is Passion, whose virtue is Love and whose vice is Jealousy. Scorpio shares the arm with it, two schools of the same fire, one weighing passion and one plunging into it.

Its classical ruler is Venus herself, and Venus lives on this very arm. Libra is thus the rare sign seated in its ruler's own house: love judged in the home of love.

variants.paint: In the paint world Libra's arm wears the deep pigment red of #B60000, a crimson with the weight of earth in it, the painter's primary. Passion becomes material: a weight set in the pan, love measured the way goods are weighed at a Friday market, heavy, warm and real.
variants.light: In the light palette the arm burns pure additive red, #FF0000. Red is a primary of light just as it was of paint, so Libra keeps its color across both worlds; only its substance changes. Screen red is not a pigment but a signal, passion as flame rather than clay, and the balance is held in a beam.

Scorpio.base:
Scorpio guards the dying of the year, 23 October to 21 November, from 120 to 150 degrees, where autumn stops pretending and the wheel sinks toward the winter tip of the dial. Its stars are drawn as a long chain that fans open at the head and hooks upward at the sting.

The owner shows the animal from above, both pincers open and raised, the plated body squared, the segmented tail curled to one side like a weapon sheathed but ready. Even the glyph is an M that ends in a barbed arrow.

It sits on the red Friday arm of Venus, mood Passion, virtue Love, vice Jealousy, and no sign knows that vice more intimately, for jealousy is love that has armed itself. Libra shares the arm: what Libra weighs, Scorpio defends.

Its classical ruler is Mars, who lives across the dial on the orange Tuesday arm. Scorpio is therefore war lodged in the house of love, passion with a soldier's memory.

variants.paint: In paint the arm is the dark pigment red of #B60000, closer to dried blood than to fire. It suits the scorpion: subtractive color works by absorbing light and giving little back, and Scorpio's passion is exactly that, taken in deeply, held, and revealed only when the tail decides.
variants.light: In the light palette the arm blazes as pure #FF0000, the first primary of the additive world. What was venom becomes voltage: passion emitted instead of absorbed, the warning coloration of the screen age, a red that does not wait in shadow but announces itself at full brightness.

Sagittarius.base:
Sagittarius takes the year's final descent, 22 November to 21 December, from 150 degrees to the bottom of the dial. Its far edge is the winter solstice: the archer walks the year into its midnight.

The owner's centaur rears on its hind legs with the bow at full draw, the arrow leveled and calm in mid-leap, aim held steady while the body plunges. Its constellation is the wheel's densest knot of stars, lines crossing like a drawn net.

It stands with Capricorn on the purple Wednesday arm of Mercury: mood Sorrow, virtue Wisdom, vice Greed. Two climbers of the dark season share that post, one aiming beyond it and one enduring through it.

Its ruler Jupiter lives on the yellow Thursday arm at the top of the dial, directly opposite, and by the star's law the opposite virtue heals the vice: Generosity is the cure for Greed. Like its glyph, the archer aims straight up at its ruler's noon.

variants.paint: In paint the arm wears purple, #542E85, red and blue pigment folded together into the dye of mourning and of kings. Sorrow becomes a worn material, and the archer's wisdom reads like Tyrian cloth: costly, dark, earned a drop at a time from the world itself.
variants.light: In the light palette the purple becomes magenta, #BD00BD, the one hue no rainbow contains, invented by the eye where the spectrum's far ends meet. A fitting midnight for an archer: he aims at a point that exists only because the mind insists on joining what nature keeps apart.

Capricorn.base:
Capricorn opens at the exact bottom of the dial. Its first point is the winter solstice, 22 December, the year's midnight beneath the silver tip, and from there to 19 January, 180 to 210 degrees, it does the hardest work on the wheel: turning descent into climb.

The owner's beast is the old sea-goat, backswept ridged horns, bearded chin, forelegs lifted mid-climb, and no hindquarters at all, only a coiled fish tail with its fluke raised like a whale sounding. Half of it climbs; half remembers the deep.

It shares the purple Wednesday arm of Mercury with Sagittarius: mood Sorrow, virtue Wisdom, vice Greed. Where the archer aims out of the dark, the goat simply keeps walking through it.

Its ruler is Saturn, keeper of limits and of time, at home on the green Saturday arm whose virtue is Patience. That placement is Capricorn's whole doctrine: the summit is never taken, it is outlasted.

variants.paint: In paint the arm holds pigment purple, #542E85, red and blue ground into the color of winter dusk and old authority. It fits the goat well: sorrow worn as a working coat, wisdom built up in layers like slow glaze, matte, dark and utterly permanent.
variants.light: The light palette turns the arm magenta, #BD00BD, the impossible color the eye conjures where red and blue light meet. The climber's midnight goes electric, sorrow with a neon edge, and the stubborn ascent is lit like a summit beacon no rainbow ever includes.

Aquarius.base:
Aquarius holds deep winter, 20 January to 18 February, the arc from 210 to 240 degrees on the dial's lower left: the small hours of the year, past midnight and leaning toward dawn, when the light grows even as the cold deepens.

The owner's figure kneels on one knee, the great jar tipped at the shoulder, and the water leaves it in scrolled curls, wave folding over wave, the doubled wave the glyph abbreviates to two zigzag lines. An air sign, yet all it owns it pours away as water.

It rests on the blue Monday arm of the Moon: mood Calm, virtue Serenity, vice Fear. Pisces shares the arm, the pourer and the swimmers, the vessel and what lives in the stream.

Its classical ruler is Saturn, at home on the green Saturday arm of Patience. That is the water-bearer's secret: the pouring looks like abandon but is discipline, a measured emptying, serenity practiced until it becomes a public work.

variants.paint: In paint the arm is primary blue, #002FFF, the lapis and indigo of the old workshops, once weighed out like treasure. Calm becomes a substance: the water-bearer pours a color painters ground by hand, and serenity settles onto the year like pigment into cloth.
variants.light: In light the arm keeps its name, for blue is a primary of both worlds, but #0040FF glows instead of covering. The poured water becomes a stream of screen-light, the beam rather than the pigment, and the bearer's calm turns luminous, a steady lamp in the dial's dark quarter.

Pisces.base:
Pisces is the zodiac's closing word, 19 February to 20 March, from 240 to 270 degrees. Its far edge lands exactly on the spring equinox, at the dial's dawn side; there the old year dissolves and hands the wheel on.

The owner draws the two fish as a closed ring, each chasing the other's tail like a current with no beginning; the glyph unbinds them into two facing crescents held by one bar. In the star chart one line ends in a small circlet of stars, a fish caught mid-turn.

It lies with Aquarius on the blue Monday arm of the Moon: mood Calm, virtue Serenity, vice Fear. The bearer pours the stream; the fish are what it carries.

Its classical ruler is Jupiter, resident of the yellow Thursday arm of Joy and Generosity. The dial gives the dreamer a benefactor: the sign that ends the year in blue calm is governed from the arm of gladness, so every ending is financed by joy.

variants.paint: Paint gives the arm true pigment blue, #002FFF, the deep working blue of water painted rather than water seen. The two fish swim in a material sea, calm laid on in slow opaque strokes, and even their fear sinks quietly into the same jar of color.
variants.light: In the light palette the arm stays blue, a primary in both worlds, but sharpens into the glowing #0040FF of a screen's own depth. The ring of fish becomes a circuit, two currents of light chasing each other, and the Moon's painted water is remade as pure signal.

zodiac_articles_note: zodiac_articles[SignName] = {base, variants}: base = 3-4 left-aligned paragraphs weaving the sign's myth, the owner's art, its season arc on the year wheel, the hexa arm it sits on (color, mood, virtue, vice), its arm partner and its classical ruler's place on the dial; variants = one paragraph each for the paint and light palettes (signs live on the hexa pointer only). Display location TBD by the owner.
